title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who discuss their involvement in charity or social responsibility initiatives. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Giving Back: Four People on Charity Work and Social Responsibility

texts.A:
### Carolyn, 52, Marketing Executive
I chair the fundraising committee for a hospice charity and have done so for six years alongside a full-time demanding job. [43]My motivation came from a personal experience – my father spent his final weeks in that hospice, and the quality of care he received was so extraordinary that I felt a genuine obligation to support the people providing it.[/43] Running a volunteer committee is far harder than I anticipated, requiring professional skills in a context where nobody is paid. [47]The most difficult part is managing volunteers with different priorities – persuasion rather than instruction is the only tool available, and it requires patience I don't always have at the end of a working week.[/47] [51]Last year's fundraising gala raised over ninety thousand pounds, and knowing that this directly funds nursing staff and specialist equipment is a more satisfying return on effort than any campaign I've managed professionally.[/51]
texts.B:
### Jerome, 34, Software Engineer
[44]I volunteer with a charity that provides digital literacy training to elderly residents who have been left behind by the shift to online services – banking, GP appointments and government forms.[/44] I work two evenings a week, and the sessions are small, unhurried and very different from the sprint culture of my professional life. [48]What has struck me most is how quickly people progress when they feel safe to ask questions they assume are obvious – the barrier to digital inclusion is often confidence rather than ability.[/48] [52]I've also recently started mentoring young people from disadvantaged backgrounds who are considering careers in technology, partly because nobody did that for me and I know how much of a difference it can make.[/52]
texts.C:
### Agnes, 68, Retired Nurse
[45]I spent forty years as a nurse, and when I retired I couldn't face the prospect of simply stopping – my professional skills felt too valuable to put away.[/45] I now volunteer once a week at a refugee health centre, providing basic health assessments and medication support. [49]What I find particularly meaningful is working with people whose trust in official institutions has been badly damaged, and showing them that healthcare here is something they are entitled to access without fear.[/49] Some of the stories I hear remind me that the difficulties in my own life have been very small by comparison. I have no plans to stop.
texts.D:
### Tobias, 28, Charity Logistics Coordinator
[46]I got involved in a local food bank during my final year at university, and what started as something I did partly to strengthen my CV has become something I genuinely couldn't imagine not doing.[/46] I help with logistics and sorting, which isn't glamorous, but I've learned that the efficiency and dignity of the operation matter enormously to the people who use it. [50]What has most changed my perspective is realising how narrow the margin between having enough and not having enough can be – several of the people I assist are in employment and still cannot afford to feed themselves adequately, which I find both shocking and motivating.[/50]

questions.question: was motivated to begin their charity work by a personal experience involving a family member?
questions.explanation: Carolyn says her motivation came from her father's final weeks in the hospice and the extraordinary care he received there.

questions.question: volunteers their professional skills to help people who are struggling to use essential digital services?
questions.explanation: Jerome provides digital literacy training to elderly residents who have been left behind by the shift to online banking, GP appointments and government forms.

questions.question: continues to apply professional expertise developed during their career for the benefit of others in retirement?
questions.explanation: Agnes says her professional skills felt too valuable to put away, so she now volunteers at a refugee health centre doing assessments and medication support.

questions.question: became involved in charitable work for partly practical reasons but found it became genuinely important to them?
questions.explanation: Tobias says what started partly to strengthen his CV has become something he genuinely couldn't imagine not doing.

questions.question: describes the challenge of managing unpaid volunteers as requiring a fundamentally different approach to leadership?
questions.explanation: Carolyn says persuasion rather than instruction is the only tool available when managing volunteers, requiring patience she doesn't always have.

questions.question: has observed that the main obstacle people face is a lack of confidence rather than a lack of ability?
questions.explanation: Jerome says people progress quickly when they feel safe to ask questions, and the barrier to digital inclusion is often confidence rather than ability.

questions.question: describes finding particular meaning in helping to rebuild trust among people who have been failed by institutions?
questions.explanation: Agnes says she finds it particularly meaningful to show refugee patients that healthcare is something they can access without fear, given their damaged trust in institutions.

questions.question: says their involvement has significantly changed their understanding of a social or economic problem?
questions.explanation: Tobias says the most significant change in his perspective is realising how narrow the margin between having enough and not having enough can be.

questions.question: describes a specific large-scale achievement that directly benefits the people an organisation exists to support?
questions.explanation: Carolyn says last year's gala raised over ninety thousand pounds, directly funding nursing staff and specialist equipment at the hospice.

questions.question: mentions an additional form of charitable contribution they make beyond their main volunteering activity?
questions.explanation: Jerome, in addition to digital literacy training, also mentors young people from disadvantaged backgrounds who are considering careers in technology.
